rcomplex 0.1.0
  • Reference
  • Articles
    • Comparative co-expression analysis with rcomplex

Comparative co-expression analysis with rcomplex

Source: vignettes/rcomplex-tutorial.Rmd
rcomplex-tutorial.Rmd

This vignette demonstrates a complete rcomplex analysis using expression data from eight Pooideae grass species spanning four phylogenetic pairs, each containing one annual and one perennial species:

Species Code Life cycle Pair
Brachypodium distachyon BDIS Annual Brachypodium
Brachypodium sylvaticum BSYL Perennial Brachypodium
Hordeum vulgare HVUL Annual Hordeum
Hordeum jubatum HJUB Perennial Hordeum
Briza maxima BMAX Annual Briza
Briza media (crossmapped to BMAX) BMED Perennial Briza
Vulpia bromoides VBRO Annual Festuca/Vulpia
Festuca pratensis FPRA Perennial Festuca/Vulpia

The dataset is a 500-HOG subset of VST-normalized RNA-seq from the “Live or let die?” project, stored as SummarizedExperiment objects.

1. Data preparation 

library(rcomplex)
library(SummarizedExperiment)

se_list <- readRDS(system.file("extdata", "pooideae_vignette.rds",
                                package = "rcomplex"))
species <- names(se_list)

# Life cycle trait
trait <- c(BDIS = "annual", BSYL = "perennial",
           HVUL = "annual", HJUB = "perennial",
           BMAX = "annual", BMED = "perennial",
           VBRO = "annual", FPRA = "perennial")

Paralog reduction

Multi-copy gene families often contain recent duplicates with nearly identical expression profiles. These inflate the combinatorial search space for clique detection without adding information. reduce_orthogroups() merges correlated paralogs within each HOG using Ward.D2 hierarchical clustering on Pearson correlation distance. Paralogs above the correlation threshold (default 0.7) are replaced by their averaged expression; subfunctionalized copies with distinct expression programs are preserved:

# Build a per-species ortholog table from rowData HOG annotations
reduced_se <- lapply(species, function(sp) {
  expr <- SummarizedExperiment::assay(se_list[[sp]], 1)
  rd <- SummarizedExperiment::rowData(se_list[[sp]])
  ortho_sp <- data.frame(
    gene = rownames(se_list[[sp]]),
    hog = as.character(rd$hog),
    stringsAsFactors = FALSE
  )
  ortho_sp <- ortho_sp[!is.na(ortho_sp$hog), ]
  reduced <- reduce_orthogroups(expr, ortho_sp, gene_col = "gene",
                                 cor_threshold = 0.7)
  cat(sp, ":", reduced$n_original, "->", reduced$n_reduced, "genes",
      "(", reduced$n_merged, "merged )\n")
  reduced
})
names(reduced_se) <- species

Network construction

Build co-expression networks from the reduced expression matrices using Mutual Rank (MR) normalization at 3% density. We pass the matrix directly since SE metadata is no longer needed post-reduction:

networks <- lapply(species, function(sp) {
  compute_network(reduced_se[[sp]]$expr_matrix,
                  norm_method = "MR", density = 0.03)
})
names(networks) <- species

Ortholog extraction

Extract ortholog pairs from HOG annotations in rowData. After paralog reduction, gene names may have changed (merged representatives), so we map through each species’ reduction. Gene IDs are species-unique (genome-specific identifiers), so each species’ map is applied only to the column containing that species’ genes:

ortho_pairs <- utils::combn(species, 2, simplify = FALSE)
ortho_list <- lapply(ortho_pairs, function(pair) {
  o <- extract_orthologs(se_list[[pair[1]]], se_list[[pair[2]]])
  o$sp1_name <- pair[1]
  o$sp2_name <- pair[2]
  o
})
orthologs <- do.call(rbind, ortho_list)

# Map original gene names to reduced representatives (species-aware)
for (sp in species) {
  gm <- reduced_se[[sp]]$gene_map
  map <- stats::setNames(gm$representative, gm$original)
  is_sp1 <- orthologs$sp1_name == sp
  is_sp2 <- orthologs$sp2_name == sp
  orthologs$Species1[is_sp1] <- ifelse(
    orthologs$Species1[is_sp1] %in% names(map),
    map[orthologs$Species1[is_sp1]], orthologs$Species1[is_sp1])
  orthologs$Species2[is_sp2] <- ifelse(
    orthologs$Species2[is_sp2] %in% names(map),
    map[orthologs$Species2[is_sp2]], orthologs$Species2[is_sp2])
}
orthologs$sp1_name <- orthologs$sp2_name <- NULL
# Deduplicate after mapping (merged paralogs collapse to same pair)
orthologs <- unique(orthologs)

cat(nrow(orthologs), "ortholog pairs across",
    length(ortho_pairs), "species pairs\n")

2. Pairwise comparison and HOG-level testing

For each of the C(8,2) = 28 species pairs, we run two analyses:

  1. Neighborhood comparison (compare_neighborhoods()): hypergeometric test for overlap of co-expression neighborhoods, producing per-pair p-values and fold-enrichment effect sizes.
  2. HOG-level permutation test (permutation_hog_test()): gene-identity permutation within each HOG. This is necessary because pair-level tests within a HOG are correlated (shared neighborhoods), making Fisher’s method or Storey q-values anti-conservative. The permutation p-value is exact regardless of the dependency structure.

With use_torch = TRUE, the fold-enrichment matrix is precomputed on GPU, reducing each permutation to fast table lookups.

The HOG-level q-values determine which edges are co-expressologs (conserved co-expression relationships):

# Helper: build edges from raw comparison + HOG-level q-values
build_perm_edges <- function(comp, hog_res, sp_a, sp_b, alpha = 0.05) {
  hog_q <- hog_res$q.value[match(comp$hog, hog_res$hog)]
  data.frame(
    gene1 = comp$Species1,
    gene2 = comp$Species2,
    species1 = sp_a,
    species2 = sp_b,
    hog = comp$hog,
    q.value = hog_q,
    effect_size = sqrt(comp$Species1.effect.size * comp$Species2.effect.size),
    type = ifelse(!is.na(hog_q) & hog_q < alpha, "conserved", "ns")
  )
}

# Run all 28 pairs
pair_edges <- list()
hog_results <- list()
idx <- 0L

for (pair in utils::combn(species, 2, simplify = FALSE)) {
  sp_a <- pair[1]; sp_b <- pair[2]

  comp <- compare_neighborhoods(networks[[sp_a]], networks[[sp_b]],
                                 orthologs)
  if (nrow(comp) == 0) next

  hog_res <- permutation_hog_test(
    networks[[sp_a]], networks[[sp_b]], comp,
    n_cores = min(4L, parallel::detectCores(logical = FALSE)),
    use_torch = TRUE
  )

  idx <- idx + 1L
  pair_edges[[idx]] <- build_perm_edges(comp, hog_res, sp_a, sp_b)
  hog_res$pair <- paste(sp_a, sp_b, sep = "-")
  hog_results[[paste(sp_a, sp_b, sep = "-")]] <- hog_res
}

edges <- do.call(rbind, pair_edges)
all_hog <- do.call(rbind, hog_results)

cat(nrow(edges), "edges:", sum(edges$type == "conserved"), "conserved,",
    sum(edges$type == "ns"), "non-significant\n")
cat(nrow(all_hog), "HOG tests,",
    sum(all_hog$q.value < 0.05), "significant (q < 0.05)\n")

For quick exploration without GPU, run_pairwise_comparisons() provides a faster analytical path using pair-level Storey q-values (less statistically rigorous for multi-copy HOGs):

edges_analytical <- run_pairwise_comparisons(networks, orthologs)
cat("Analytical:", sum(edges_analytical$type == "conserved"), "conserved edges\n")
cat("Permutation:", sum(edges$type == "conserved"), "conserved edges\n")

4. Module-level analysis

Detect co-expression modules with Leiden community detection, then compare across species:

mod_bdis <- detect_modules(networks$BDIS, method = "leiden", resolution = 1.0)
mod_bsyl <- detect_modules(networks$BSYL, method = "leiden", resolution = 1.0)

mod_comp <- compare_modules(mod_bdis, mod_bsyl, orthologs,
                             method = "hypergeometric")
classes <- classify_modules(mod_comp)
print(table(classes$classification))

5. Edge robustness: density sweep

Network density controls how many edges are retained in the co-expression network. At 3% density, only the top 3% of all pairwise MR values pass the threshold – these are the strongest co-expression relationships.

We sweep over 11 density levels centered at the 3% baseline, testing both directions:

  • Tighter (below 3%): does the signal survive stricter thresholds? Edges that persist are strong, not marginal.
  • Looser (above 3%): is the signal specific to this density, or stable across a range? Cliques that only appear at very loose thresholds are likely noise from extra edges.

The 3% choice is somewhat arbitrary. Centering the sweep around it reveals sensitivity to that choice – a truly robust co-expressolog should be present across a range of densities, not just at one particular cutoff.

The permutation pipeline is re-run at each level because network neighborhoods change with the threshold, altering the HOG-level q-values:

# 11 levels: 5 looser + baseline + 5 tighter
multipliers <- seq(0.95, 1.05, by = 0.01)
sweep_results <- list()

for (m in multipliers) {
  tight_nets <- lapply(networks, function(net) {
    list(network = net$network, threshold = net$threshold * m)
  })
  names(tight_nets) <- species

  # Effective density at this threshold
  eff_density <- mean(vapply(species, function(sp) {
    vals <- networks[[sp]]$network[upper.tri(networks[[sp]]$network)]
    mean(vals >= networks[[sp]]$threshold * m)
  }, numeric(1)))

  # Re-run comparisons + permutation at stricter threshold
  tight_pair_edges <- list()
  tidx <- 0L
  for (pair in utils::combn(species, 2, simplify = FALSE)) {
    sp_a <- pair[1]; sp_b <- pair[2]
    comp <- tryCatch(
      compare_neighborhoods(tight_nets[[sp_a]], tight_nets[[sp_b]], orthologs),
      error = function(e) NULL)
    if (is.null(comp) || nrow(comp) == 0) next
    hog_res <- permutation_hog_test(tight_nets[[sp_a]], tight_nets[[sp_b]],
                                     comp,
                                     n_cores = min(4L, parallel::detectCores(logical = FALSE)),
                                     use_torch = TRUE)
    tidx <- tidx + 1L
    tight_pair_edges[[tidx]] <- build_perm_edges(comp, hog_res, sp_a, sp_b)
  }
  tight_edges <- if (tidx > 0) do.call(rbind, tight_pair_edges[seq_len(tidx)])
                 else edges[0, ]

  n_conserved <- sum(tight_edges$type == "conserved")
  sweep_results[[as.character(m)]] <- list(
    edges = tight_edges, eff_density = eff_density,
    n_conserved = n_conserved)
}

# Edge survival across density levels (baseline = 3% at multiplier 1.0)
cat("Multiplier | Eff. density | Conserved edges\n")
cat("-----------|--------------|----------------\n")
for (m in names(sweep_results)) {
  r <- sweep_results[[m]]
  label <- if (as.numeric(m) == 1) " *baseline*" else ""
  cat(sprintf("%-10s | %12s | %15d%s\n",
              paste0(m, "x"),
              sprintf("%.2f%%", r$eff_density * 100),
              r$n_conserved, label))
}

The edge count may not decrease monotonically because the permutation test operates at the HOG level: tighter neighborhoods can sharpen the signal for some HOGs while eliminating it for others. Local fluctuations reflect the discrete nature of the permutation q-values. The interpretation is asymmetric: surviving tighter thresholds indicates strong signal, while surviving looser thresholds indicates specificity (not merely noise from additional edges).

Analytical alternative: clique_persistence(). The density sweep above is rigorous but expensive — it re-runs the full permutation pipeline at each threshold level. For a quick estimate, clique_persistence() computes the ratio of each co-expressolog edge’s MR value to the species’ density threshold. A persistence of 1.5 means the weakest supporting edge is 50% above threshold and would survive a 1.5x tightening. This is a principled analytical proxy: it correctly identifies the weakest link in each clique’s co-expressolog support. However, it cannot detect that tighter neighborhoods may change which HOGs reach significance in the permutation test, or that new cliques may form from HOGs that become significant at stricter thresholds. Use persistence for screening or when GPU is unavailable; use the full sweep for publication-quality robustness assessment.

6. Clique detection and phylogenetic stability

Using edges from a robust density level (1.02x), we find cliques and test their phylogenetic stability via leave-k-out jackknife. The jackknife removes k species at a time and checks whether each trait-exclusive clique is preserved:

# Use the baseline (1.0x) edges for clique detection and stability
robust_edges <- sweep_results[["1"]]$edges
cat("Using", sum(robust_edges$type == "conserved"), "conserved edges at",
    sprintf("%.2f%%", sweep_results[["1"]]$eff_density * 100), "density\n\n")

# Find cliques
cliques <- find_cliques(robust_edges, species, min_species = 2L)
cat(nrow(cliques), "cliques\n")
print(table(cliques$n_species))

# Jackknife stability (all k from 1 to N-2)
max_k <- length(species) - 2L
stab <- clique_stability(robust_edges, species, trait,
                          all_species = species,
                          full_cliques = cliques,
                          max_k = max_k)
cat("\nTrait-exclusive cliques:", length(stab$stability_class), "\n")
cat("Stability class distribution:\n")
print(table(factor(stab$stability_class, levels = 0:max_k)))

7. HOG classification with robustness ranking

Classify each HOG by its conservation pattern, then rank within each category by robustness. The waterfall assigns each HOG to exactly one class: complete > partial > differentiated > trait_specific > unclassified.

result <- classify_cliques(robust_edges, species, trait,
                            min_species = 2L,
                            stability = stab)
print(table(result$classification))
  • complete: all 8 species form a clique – deeply conserved across all Pooideae
  • partial: clique spans both annual and perennial species, but not all 8
  • differentiated: annual species have a clique AND perennial species have a clique, but no cross-group conservation – lineage-specific co-expression programs
  • trait_specific: only annuals or only perennials have a clique
  • unclassified: no conservation signal detected

The robustness ranking comes from combining density sweep survival with stability class. HOGs classified at a stricter density level with high stability class are the strongest findings:

# Count how many density levels each HOG's edges survive
hog_edge_survival <- cbind(vapply(names(sweep_results), function(m) {
  e <- sweep_results[[m]]$edges
  conserved_hogs <- unique(e$hog[e$type == "conserved"])
  result$hog %in% conserved_hogs
}, logical(nrow(result))))
result$n_densities_survived <- rowSums(hog_edge_survival)

# Show top-ranked HOGs per category
for (cls in c("complete", "partial", "differentiated", "trait_specific")) {
  sub <- result[result$classification == cls, ]
  if (nrow(sub) == 0) next
  sort_q <- if ("best_mean_q" %in% names(sub)) sub$best_mean_q else 0
  sub <- sub[order(-sub$n_densities_survived, sort_q), ]
  cat("\n", toupper(cls), "- top 5:\n")
  show_cols <- c("hog", "n_species", "best_mean_q", "stability_class",
                 "n_densities_survived")
  show_cols <- intersect(show_cols, names(sub))
  print(head(sub[, show_cols], 5), row.names = FALSE)
}

sessionInfo()
